---
title: "12.3 Rust array libraries and memory order"
---

## Explanation

Rust's standard library gives you `Vec<T>` and slices such as `&[T]` and
`&mut [T]`. For multidimensional numerical arrays, you usually choose a crate.
There is no single correct choice for every project. Choose based on the
calculation, dependencies, memory layout, and whether you need linear algebra,
automatic differentiation, or GPU execution.

One established choice is [`ndarray`](https://docs.rs/ndarray/). It provides
n-dimensional arrays, views, slicing, iteration, and elementwise operations. Its
default memory layout is row-major, also called C order. This means the
rightmost index varies fastest in memory.

For linear algebra on `ndarray` arrays, a common companion crate is
[`ndarray-linalg`](https://github.com/rust-ndarray/ndarray-linalg), which uses
external LAPACK implementations. This can be useful when you want a
Rust/`ndarray` interface to BLAS/LAPACK-style routines. Before choosing it for
a project, check the current release, backend requirements, open issues, and
maintenance activity.

Another developing option is
[`tenferro-rs`](https://github.com/tensor4all/tenferro-rs), a tensor4all Rust
tensor stack for scientific computing. It is being developed for typed tensor
computation, CPU/GPU backends, automatic differentiation, traced graphs, linear
algebra, einsum, and FFT. It follows column-major conventions, closer to
Fortran, MATLAB, and Julia. It is pre-1.0, so APIs and crate boundaries may
change.

The memory order matters. A 2 by 3 array filled from the sequence
`1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6` is interpreted differently in row-major and column-major
order:

```text
row-major:
1 2 3
4 5 6

column-major:
1 3 5
2 4 6
```

When translating Julia code to Rust, do not assume the array library uses the
same memory order as Julia. Write down the intended shape, indexing convention,
and memory layout before asking an AI agent to implement array code.

## Things to look up

- `ndarray`
- `ndarray-linalg`
- `tenferro-rs`
- Row-major order
- Column-major order
- BLAS/LAPACK

## Exercise

Start from a Julia loop where memory access order matters. For example, sum all
elements of a matrix with the column-major-friendly loop order:

```julia
function sum_matrix(A)
    s = 0.0
    n, m = size(A)
    for j in 1:m
        for i in 1:n
            s += A[i, j]
        end
    end
    return s
end
```

Ask an AI agent to rewrite this calculation in Rust using an array library of
your choice. Before it writes code, ask it to state the memory layout of the
chosen library.

Implement two Rust versions:

- one version that respects the chosen memory order,
- one version that intentionally uses the less favorable loop order.

Measure the runtime for both versions on the same array size. The benchmark can
be simple, but it must use the same data and repeat the calculation enough
times that the timing difference is meaningful.

Write a short note explaining which version was faster and why. If the result
does not match your expectation, explain what you checked.

## Notes for the exercise

- Check current documentation and repository activity before choosing a crate.
- If translating Julia array code, pay special attention to column-major versus
  row-major layout.
- With `ndarray`'s default row-major layout, the rightmost index should vary
  fastest for contiguous access.
- With a column-major library, the leftmost index should vary fastest for
  contiguous access.
- If using `ndarray-linalg`, check which BLAS/LAPACK backend is required.
- If using `tenferro-rs`, remember that it is pre-1.0 and may change.
- Verify that the two Rust versions compute the same numerical result before
  comparing speed.
- The goal is not to prove that one library is always faster. The goal is to
  see that memory layout is part of the numerical specification.
